templates.title: Build vs. Buy Decision Memo
templates.summary: Structured memo for build-vs-buy-vs-status-quo decisions on capabilities requiring multi-engineer-quarter investment. Forces comparable analysis: full TCO, time-to-value, strategic ownership, reversibility, and the often-skipped status quo option.
templates.why_it_matters: Most build-vs-buy decisions go wrong because build cost is under-estimated (initial dev is a fraction of TCO), buy cost is over-estimated (sticker price isn't TCO), 'strategic' justification papers over weak quantitative analysis, and the option to stay status quo is never seriously evaluated. This memo forces honest comparable analysis across all three.
when_to_use: Decision about a capability requiring 6+ months of engineering effort if built
Vendor decision that would commit 3+ years of operating cost
Platform vs. point solution evaluations
Strategic capability where 'we should just build it' instinct is loud
not_for: Trivial decisions (small features, minor tooling)
Cases where status quo is genuinely impossible (must do something now)
Bypassing security/legal review of vendor decisions
source_inspiration: Standard enterprise architecture build-vs-buy frameworks
Will Larson — Staff Engineer on platform investment decisions
FinOps Foundation — TCO patterns
Marc Andreessen — software is eating the world (and most non-core software is buy)

inputs_required.name: Capability description
inputs_required.description: What we're deciding about, who consumes it, strategic relevance, maturity of need.

inputs_required.name: Honest TCO data
inputs_required.description: Build cost includes 3-year operating; buy cost includes implementation + ongoing + exit.

inputs_required.name: Vendor finalists (if buy is on table)
Status quo cost
inputs_required.description: Cost of doing without, including the compounding workaround tax.

sections.name: The capability
sections.purpose: Define what we're deciding about and why.

sections.name: The three options
sections.purpose: Build, buy, and status quo — each evaluated comparably.
fields: Option A — Build (scope, initial cost, ongoing cost, TCO, time-to-value, capabilities owned/lacked, risks)
Option B — Buy (vendor candidates, TCO, time-to-value, capabilities gained/lacked, risks)
Option C — Status quo (what it means, current cost, 3-year projection, when it breaks, risks)

sections.name: Comparison and recommendation
sections.purpose: Side-by-side; reversibility; strategic vs. commodity check; recommendation.
fields: Recommendation with rationale tied to the analysis

sections.name: Implementation logistics
sections.purpose: If build or buy, the next-step logistics.
fields: If building: lead, team, phasing, success criteria, sunset plan
If buying: vendor selection, implementation owner, integration scope, success criteria, exit plan

sections.name: Common failure modes
sections.purpose: Pre-empt the ways these decisions go wrong.
fields: Build cost under-estimated (operating is the dominant cost)
'Strategic' used to justify weak quantitative case

templates.template_body_markdown:
# Build vs. Buy Decision Memo

## How to use

This memo structures the decision to build a capability internally vs. buy it from a vendor (or stay status quo). It's intended for capabilities that are non-trivial — typically multi-engineer-quarter to deliver — where the decision is committing 6+ months of engineering effort and 3+ years of operating cost.

The memo's job is not to advocate; it's to force comparable analysis across options so the decision can be made on the merits. Most build-vs-buy decisions go wrong because:

- Build cost is under-estimated (initial dev is a fraction of TCO)
- Buy cost is over-estimated (sticker price isn't TCO)
- "Strategic" justification papers over weak quantitative analysis
- Optionality cost (what if we change our minds in 2 years?) is ignored

Fill out all sections honestly. Include "stay status quo" as a real option, not a strawman.

---

## Header

- **Decision**: build / buy / stay status quo for [capability]
- **Owner**: 
- **Decision deadline**: 
- **Decision-maker / approver**: 
- **Estimated investment**: 
- **Time horizon for analysis** (typically 3 years): 

---

## Part 1: The capability

### 1.1 What capability are we deciding about

- **Capability description**: 
- **Users / consumers internally**: 
- **Strategic relevance**: how core is this to our differentiated value? (Core / supporting / commodity)
- **Maturity of need**: how stable is the requirement? (Stable / evolving / experimental)

### 1.2 Why are we deciding now

- **Trigger**: 
- **Cost of NOT deciding**: what continues to break or be missed without this decision

---

## Part 2: The three options

### Option A — Build internally

- **Scope of build**: what we'd actually build (MVP and target state)
- **Initial development cost** (engineer-weeks, lead time, $)
- **Ongoing operating cost** (engineering time per quarter to maintain, evolve, support)
- **3-year TCO**: 
- **Time-to-value**: when we'd have the capability live
- **Capabilities we'd own**: data, customization, IP, evolution control
- **Capabilities we'd lack vs. buy**: maturity gaps relative to a market-leading vendor
- **Risks**: scope creep, key-person dependency, missed market shifts, opportunity cost on team's other work

### Option B — Buy (vendor)

- **Vendor candidates**: 1–3 finalists if known
- **Total Cost of Ownership** (license + implementation + ongoing operations + escape cost)
  - Year 1: 
  - Years 2–3: 
  - 3-year TCO: 
- **Time-to-value**: when we'd have the capability live
- **Capabilities gained immediately**: maturity, ecosystem, ongoing innovation
- **Capabilities we'd lack vs. build**: customization, data ownership, integration depth
- **Risks**: vendor lock-in, vendor financial health, integration complexity, security/compliance

### Option C — Stay status quo

- **What status quo means**: doing without, manual workarounds, partial coverage
- **Cost of status quo**: ongoing cost of the workaround (engineering time, customer impact, operational risk)
- **3-year cost projection** (the workaround compounds; quantify): 
- **When status quo breaks**: at what scale, growth, or strategic shift this stops being viable
- **Risks**: what happens if status quo runs out before we revisit the decision

---

## Part 3: Comparison and recommendation

### 3.1 Side-by-side

| Dimension | Build | Buy | Status quo |
|-----------|-------|-----|------------|
| Year-1 cost |  |  |  |
| 3-year TCO |  |  |  |
| Time-to-value |  |  |  |
| Strategic ownership |  |  |  |
| Optionality (can we reverse in year 2?) |  |  |  |
| Capability ceiling |  |  |  |
| Risk profile |  |  |  |

### 3.2 Reversibility analysis

- **Build → Switch later**: cost and time to switch from a build to a vendor in year 2 (often high)
- **Buy → Switch later**: cost and time to switch vendors or migrate to build in year 2 (depends on lock-in)
- **One-way doors vs. two-way doors**: which option locks us in, which preserves optionality

### 3.3 Strategic vs. commodity question

- **Is this our differentiator?**: if the capability IS the differentiator, building usually right; if it's commodity, buying usually right
- **Honest assessment**: most teams overclaim the strategic relevance of work they want to build; check this honestly

### 3.4 Recommendation

- **Recommendation**: build / buy / status quo (with vendor name if buy)
- **Rationale tied to the side-by-side**: not advocacy; show the analysis
- **What we're explicitly accepting**: the trade-offs of the recommended option
- **What would change the recommendation**: conditions under which we'd revisit

---

## Part 4: Implementation logistics (if build or buy)

### If building

- **Lead engineer / architect**: 
- **Team**: 
- **Phasing**: MVP, then evolution
- **Success criteria** (when we'd say the build worked): 
- **Sunset plan if build fails**: when we'd accept it isn't working and pivot to buy

### If buying

- **Vendor selection process**: see `vendor-evaluation-pack`
- **Implementation owner**: 
- **Integration scope**: 
- **Success criteria** (when we'd say the buy worked): 
- **Exit plan if vendor fails**: when we'd switch vendors or rebuild

---

## Part 5: Approval

- **Approver**: 
- **Date approved**: 
- **Approval conditions** (any specific commitments tied to approval): 

---

## Common failure modes

- **Build cost under-estimated**: initial development is a fraction of total cost. Include 3-year operating cost (engineers maintaining, evolving, supporting); typically 30–70% of initial dev cost annually.
- **"Strategic" used to justify weak quantitative case**: if the build is strategic, it should still survive a quantitative comparison. If it doesn't, it's not strategic enough to build.
- **Status quo not seriously evaluated**: most teams skip status quo because it's not exciting; often it's the right answer for 1–2 more years while the org figures out the real strategic shape.
- **Optionality ignored**: locking into a build in year 1 that won't be revisitable in year 3 even if better options emerge.
- **Vendor TCO doesn't include exit cost**: if you commit to a vendor, the cost of leaving is part of the cost.
- **Analyst doing the analysis is also the builder**: builder bias toward building. Get an outside reviewer on the analysis.

---

## Related artifacts

- `vendor-evaluation-pack` (prompts) — for the buy-side vendor selection
- `platform-investment-case` (template) — for the build-side investment case
- `roi-business-case` (template) — for the broader ROI framing
- `tco-narrative` (in `vendor-evaluation-pack`) — for the TCO calculation discipline

templates.title: Pre-Mortem Template
templates.summary: Facilitator's template for running a pre-mortem on a plan, strategy, or initiative — before commitment. Imagining the failed future and working backward to surface predictable failure modes the optimistic planning conversation missed.
templates.why_it_matters: Pre-mortems are the cheapest insurance on a strategic plan. They surface failure modes that the regular planning conversation systematically misses because the room is biased toward optimism, politeness, or sunk-cost momentum. Skipping them means the predictable failures hit and the team is surprised.
when_to_use: Before locking a quarterly plan or strategic commitment
Before launching a major initiative (>$1M cost or 10+ engineer-weeks)
Before committing to a customer-promised roadmap item with high downside
When the team's optimism feels disconnected from the team's track record
not_for: Plans already executing (run a mid-flight check instead)
Trivial decisions (a feature, not a strategy)
Cases where the plan owner is not open to adjusting the plan (pre-mortem becomes performative)
source_inspiration: Gary Klein — original pre-mortem methodology (Harvard Business Review)
Daniel Kahneman — Thinking Fast and Slow on planning fallacy
Annie Duke — Thinking in Bets on probabilistic decision-making

inputs_required.name: Plan to pre-mortem
inputs_required.description: The committed-or-about-to-be-committed plan with success criteria.

inputs_required.name: 4–10 participants
inputs_required.description: Plan owner, direct executors, adjacent skeptics, domain veterans, fresh eyes.

inputs_required.name: Separate facilitator
inputs_required.description: NOT the plan owner — their presence suppresses critique.

inputs_required.name: 90-minute meeting
inputs_required.description: Discrete time block; not folded into another meeting.

outputs: Decisions to make NOW with owners and deadlines

sections.name: Pre-meeting setup (facilitator owns)
sections.purpose: Set up the conditions for honest critique.
fields: Facilitator prep (read plan, identify gloss areas, brief owner, brief participants, set no-rebuttal rule)

sections.name: The meeting (90 minutes)
sections.purpose: Imagine the failed future; surface specific failure modes; rank; identify decisions.
fields: Phase 1 — Frame the failed future (5 min)
Phase 2 — Silent generation of failure modes (15 min)
Phase 4 — Cluster and rank by likelihood × impact (20 min)
Phase 5 — Hidden dependencies and single points of failure (10 min)
Phase 6 — Decisions to make NOW with owners and deadlines (10 min)

sections.name: Post-meeting synthesis (24 hours)
sections.purpose: Capture the output; force the plan to change (or risks to be explicitly accepted).
fields: Plan adjustments recommended (cuts, phasing, mitigations, acceptances)
Plan changes the owner has agreed to

sections.name: Common failure modes
sections.purpose: Pre-empt the ways pre-mortems become performative.
fields: No decisions on the way out

templates.estimated_time: 30 min facilitator prep; 90 min meeting; 60 min synthesis = 3 hours total
templates.template_body_markdown:
# Pre-Mortem Template

## How to use

A pre-mortem is the inverse of a postmortem. Before committing to a plan, the team imagines a future in which the plan has clearly failed, then works backward to identify what predictably went wrong.

It's the cheapest insurance you can buy on a strategic plan, a quarterly commitment, or a major initiative. It surfaces predictable failure modes that the regular planning conversation misses because the room is biased toward optimism (or politeness, or sunk-cost momentum).

Run a pre-mortem **before** the commitment is locked. Hour-and-a-half meeting, 4–10 people, a facilitator who isn't the plan's owner. The facilitator's job is to keep the room in the failed-future frame and prevent the conversation from drifting back to "but we'll handle that."

This template covers facilitation prep, the meeting itself, and the post-meeting synthesis.

---

## Part 1: Pre-meeting setup (facilitator owns)

### 1.1 Plan to be pre-mortemed

- **Plan / initiative being pre-mortemed**: 
- **Owner**: 
- **Time horizon**: 
- **Stated success criteria** (what the plan claims will be true at the end): 
- **Pre-read sent to participants** (1-page plan summary): 

### 1.2 Participants

Pick 4–10 people across these categories:

- **Plan owner**: yes (will mostly listen, defend less, listen more)
- **Direct executors** (will be doing the work): 2–3
- **Adjacent skeptics** (people who'd be affected but aren't on the team): 1–2
- **Domain veterans** (have seen this kind of plan before): 1–2
- **Outside fresh eyes** (no investment in the plan): 1–2
- **Facilitator**: separate person, NOT the plan owner

### 1.3 Facilitator's prep

- [ ] Read the plan in full
- [ ] Identify 2–3 areas where you suspect the plan glosses (helpful as backup probe questions)
- [ ] Brief the plan owner on their job: listen, take notes, defend nothing in the room
- [ ] Brief participants on the framing: "Imagine it's [end date]. The plan failed clearly and visibly. Why?"
- [ ] Set a no-rebuttal rule for the room

---

## Part 2: The meeting (90 minutes)

### Phase 1 — Frame the failed future (5 min)

The facilitator opens with a specific scenario:

> "It's [end date]. We're doing the postmortem on this plan. It failed — the success criteria were not met, the executive review was bad, and the team is doing damage assessment. For the next 90 minutes, we're working backward from that failure to identify why."

Two ground rules:
1. **No rebuttals.** "But we'll mitigate that" is not allowed in the room. Capture the failure mode; mitigate later.
2. **Specificity.** "People left" is not a failure mode. "Maya left in week 3 and the iOS migration lost its only senior reviewer" is a failure mode.

### Phase 2 — Silent generation (15 min)

Each participant writes failure modes silently. Targets:
- Minimum 5 failure modes per person
- Each is specific (named people, projects, events, dates)
- Each is plausible (not "the office burns down")

The facilitator's role here is to keep the room quiet. Do not begin discussing.

### Phase 3 — Round-robin (30 min)

Each participant reads ONE failure mode at a time, rotating. Facilitator captures verbatim. No discussion until all are surfaced.

This phase usually surfaces 30–60 failure modes. Don't filter; capture all.

### Phase 4 — Cluster and rank (20 min)

Group failure modes into clusters (e.g., "key person leaves," "dependency slips," "scope explodes," "wrong product-market fit"). Then rank clusters by:
- **Likelihood**: how likely in the time horizon (rare / possible / likely / expected)
- **Impact**: if it happens, how bad (minor / material / fatal)

Identify the top 3–5 clusters by likelihood × impact.

### Phase 5 — Hidden dependencies and single points of failure (10 min)

Specific question to surface what the plan rests on:
- "What does this plan secretly depend on that we haven't named?"
- Common: a single person, a partner team's commitment, an external event, an unreviewed decision.

### Phase 6 — Decisions to make NOW (10 min)

For each top cluster, the facilitator asks:
- "What decision could we make in the next week that would meaningfully reduce this risk?"
- Capture the decision, the owner, and the deadline.

The plan owner doesn't have to commit in the room — but every decision should have a named owner and a date for the call.

---

## Part 3: Post-meeting synthesis (24 hours)

The facilitator writes the pre-mortem report:

### 3.1 Top 5 failure clusters (ranked)

For each:
- **Cluster name**: 
- **Specific failure modes** (the verbatim ones from the meeting): 
- **Likelihood × impact rationale**: 
- **Hidden dependency / single point of failure** (if any): 

### 3.2 Decisions to make NOW

| # | Decision | Owner | Deadline | Status |
|---|----------|-------|----------|--------|
| 1 |          |       |          |        |
| 2 |          |       |          |        |

### 3.3 Plan adjustments recommended

- **Cuts** (parts of the plan that should be removed to reduce risk): 
- **Phasing** (work that should happen in a different order): 
- **Mitigations** (specific work added to reduce risk of top clusters): 
- **Acceptance** (risks the team accepts as-is, with explicit acknowledgment): 

### 3.4 Plan changes the owner has agreed to

The owner reviews and either accepts or pushes back, in writing. The pre-mortem only adds value if the plan changes (or the team explicitly accepts the risks).

### 3.5 Scheduled re-check

When (typically a third of the way through the plan) we re-check: are any of the top failure clusters showing early signs?

---

## Common failure modes (in the pre-mortem itself)

- **Plan owner runs the meeting**: their presence subtly suppresses honest critique. Get a separate facilitator.
- **Rebuttals allowed**: "but we'll handle that" rationalizes away the value. The facilitator must enforce no-rebuttal.
- **Generic failure modes**: "communication breaks down," "team is too small." These are useless. Force specificity.
- **No decisions on the way out**: a pre-mortem without decisions is therapy. The Phase 6 step is non-negotiable.
- **No re-check scheduled**: the predicted failure happens in week 7 and no one was watching.
- **Pre-mortem becomes performative**: if the team doesn't believe their input changes the plan, they stop bringing real failure modes. Run pre-mortems only when you genuinely intend to adjust.

---

## When NOT to run a pre-mortem

- The plan is already executing (run a mid-flight check instead)
- The team has already done a thorough risk review (don't duplicate)
- The plan owner is not open to changing the plan (pre-mortem will be performative)
- For trivial decisions (don't pre-mortem a feature; pre-mortem a strategy or a quarter)

---

## Related artifacts

- `pre-mortem-facilitator-pack` (prompts) — for facilitating the meeting and synthesizing the output
- `quarterly-planning-packet` — the plan that often warrants a pre-mortem
- `risk-register` — where the surviving risks land for ongoing tracking
- `dependency-risk-review` — for the hidden-dependency portion at depth

templates.template_body_markdown:
# Capacity Forecasting Model

## How to use

This template structures a multi-quarter capacity forecast for an engineering organization or platform. The output is an honest projection of demand vs. supply across compute, storage, network, vendors, and engineering capacity — with the trigger points where you'll need to invest, optimize, or change direction.

Most teams forecast capacity reactively: a service hits a wall, an alert fires, someone scrambles. This template flips that to anticipatory: when will we hit which limit, what's the lead time to address it, when do we need to start.

This is for capacity types where the lead time to act is material (weeks to quarters): cloud capacity in specific regions, vendor seat licenses, engineering headcount, specialized hardware. For elastic scaling that responds in seconds, this template is overkill — that's an autoscaling configuration, not a forecast.

---

## Header

- **Forecast period**: typically next 4–8 quarters
- **Owner**: 
- **Last forecast**: 
- **Next refresh**: typically quarterly
- **Approval / review path**: 

---

## Part 1: What we're forecasting

For each capacity dimension, define:

| Dimension | Unit | Current usage | Trend (% / quarter) | Projected demand 4Q out |
|-----------|------|---------------|---------------------|-------------------------|
| Cloud compute (region X) |  |  |  |  |
| Cloud storage |  |  |  |  |
| Network egress |  |  |  |  |
| Database (transactions / sec) |  |  |  |  |
| Vendor license seats |  |  |  |  |
| Engineering headcount (by team / skill) |  |  |  |  |
| Specialized hardware (GPU, etc.) |  |  |  |  |

---

## Part 2: Demand drivers

What's driving demand growth or shrinkage:

- **User / customer growth**: rate of new sign-ups, expected for next 4Q
- **Usage growth per user**: feature adoption, deeper usage patterns
- **New product launches**: capacity demand each launch creates
- **Strategic shifts**: planned changes that materially alter demand
- **Seasonality**: predictable cycles (e.g., Q4 retail load, end-of-quarter sales spike)
- **One-time events**: marketing campaigns, partnership launches, conferences

For each, the impact on each capacity dimension and the timing.

---

## Part 3: Supply constraints

What's capping supply:

- **Hard limits**: regional capacity ceilings, license maximums, hardware lead times
- **Cost ceilings**: budget constraints that cap growth before infrastructure does
- **Vendor lead times**: how long to onboard / scale specific vendors
- **Hiring lead time**: typically 3–6 months from req approval to first contribution
- **Architectural limits**: places where current architecture won't scale beyond N (often the most consequential)

---

## Part 4: Forecast scenarios

Three scenarios, not one:

### Base case (~50% likely)

- Demand drivers play out as expected
- Supply expands on planned cadence
- **When we hit which limit**: per dimension, the projected month
- **Investment decisions needed by**: lead-time-adjusted dates

### Optimistic case (~25% likely)

- Demand grows faster than expected (good problem)
- **What breaks first**: the dimensions most sensitive to growth
- **Investment decisions accelerated**: dates pulled in
- **What we'd cut to make room**: if budget-constrained

### Pessimistic case (~25% likely)

- Demand grows slower OR a constraint hits earlier than expected
- **Capacity overbuilt**: where we'd see waste
- **Cost containment options**: what we'd pull back
- **Strategic implications**: if growth stalls, what that means for the team

---

## Part 5: Decision triggers

For each capacity dimension, the trigger points:

| Dimension | Current | Yellow trigger | Red trigger | Action at trigger |
|-----------|---------|----------------|-------------|-------------------|
|           |         |                |             |                   |

- **Yellow**: start planning the action (typically at 60–70% of capacity)
- **Red**: action must be in flight (typically at 80–90% of capacity)
- **Action**: specific, with lead time accounted for

---

## Part 6: Investment / optimization options

Per dimension, the levers available:

### Cloud capacity
- **Add capacity** (lead time, cost)
- **Optimize usage** (efficiency project, lead time)
- **Multi-region distribution** (architecture work)
- **Reserved instances / commitments** (cost reduction at scale)

### Engineering headcount
- **Hire** (3–6 month lead time; cost; risk)
- **Contractor bridge** (faster but expensive; bridging only)
- **Reallocate from lower-priority work** (immediate; cost is opportunity)
- **Outsource specific work** (variable; risk)

### Vendor capacity
- **Renegotiate** (timing tied to renewal)
- **Add seats** (typically fast)
- **Replace** (long lead time; switching cost)

---

## Part 7: Risk register

| Risk | Likelihood | Impact | Mitigation |
|------|-----------|--------|------------|
| Demand grows 2x base case |  |  |  |
| Hiring slows below plan |  |  |  |
| Architectural ceiling hit before forecast |  |  |  |
| Vendor changes pricing materially |  |  |  |
| Regional capacity unavailable when needed |  |  |  |

---

## Part 8: Decisions to make this quarter

From the forecast, what we need to decide NOW (not later):

- **Decisions**: list with deadline
- **Owners**: 
- **What enables each decision** (data, alignment, approval): 

---

## Common failure modes

- **Single-scenario forecast**: one number doesn't help; it's the variance that matters.
- **No lead time accounting**: hitting yellow at 70% capacity is fine if action takes 2 weeks; deadly if action takes 6 months.
- **Architectural limits ignored**: the assumption that infrastructure is elastic; specific architectures have hard ceilings the forecast must call out.
- **Forecast made once, never updated**: capacity forecasts are quarterly minimum; faster if growth is volatile.
- **Disconnect from financial planning**: capacity drives spend; forecast must be reconciled with the budget.
- **Hidden demand drivers**: a partnership signed by Sales that adds load; a marketing campaign no one told engineering about. Forecast assumes you have the demand picture; cross-check with Sales/Marketing.

---

## Related artifacts

- `engineering-budget-planner` — for the financial side of capacity
- `cloud-spend-review` — for cloud-specific capacity and cost
- `headcount-business-case` — for the headcount portion
- `capacity-forecasting-pack` (prompts) — for facilitating forecast updates

This template forces the work that turns 'we have backups' into a real recovery capability.
when_to_use: Annual DR/BCP review with stakeholders
Quarterly tabletop exercise
After significant architecture changes (new vendors, region expansion, new critical services)
After a near-miss or related incident that surfaced a gap
not_for: Routine incident response (use incident-review-packet for individual incidents)
Replacing actual testing — the plan is theory until tested
Untested backup verification (test or it's not a backup)

templates.template_body_markdown:
# Disaster Recovery & Business Continuity Plan (DR/BCP)

## How to use

This template structures a DR/BCP plan for an engineering organization — recovery from major disruptions like region-wide cloud outages, ransomware events, data corruption, or extended provider failures. It's NOT for routine incidents (use the incident response runbooks for those); it's for the events that test whether the company can continue to operate.

Most teams have *some* DR thinking — backups exist, on-call is set up. Few have actually tested the plan end-to-end, identified the dependencies that aren't recoverable, or distinguished what they'd recover from what they'd accept losing.

This is intended as a living document, reviewed annually with stakeholders and tested at least quarterly via a tabletop exercise (not just paper review).

---

## Header

- **Plan owner**: 
- **Last reviewed**: 
- **Next scheduled review**: 
- **Last tested (tabletop)**: 
- **Last tested (live drill)**: 
- **Approver / sign-off**: 

---

## Part 1: Recovery objectives

### 1.1 RTO and RPO per critical service

For each critical service or capability, define:

- **RTO (Recovery Time Objective)**: how long until the service must be restored
- **RPO (Recovery Point Objective)**: how much data loss is acceptable (typically the time window between backups)
- **Justification**: why these targets (customer commitment, SLA, business continuity needs)

| Service | RTO | RPO | Tier | Justification |
|---------|-----|-----|------|---------------|
|         |     |     |      |               |

Tier definitions (typical):
- **Tier 0**: cannot be down at all (rare; usually customer-facing core)
- **Tier 1**: down up to 1 hour acceptable; recover same day
- **Tier 2**: down up to 1 day acceptable
- **Tier 3**: down up to 1 week acceptable

### 1.2 What we'd accept losing

Be honest. Some data, some services, some capabilities are not worth the cost of full recovery. Document explicitly:

- **Data we won't recover (or only after extended delay)**:
- **Services we'd run in degraded mode for an extended period**:
- **Capabilities we'd suspend during recovery**:

---

## Part 2: Disaster scenarios

For each scenario, document the response. Cover at minimum:

### 2.1 Cloud region outage (extended, multi-hour)

- **Detection**: how we'd know, alerting in place
- **Failover**: where we'd shift traffic, capacity
- **Communication**: status page, customer notification, internal
- **Estimated RTO**: 
- **Known gaps**: 

### 2.2 Cloud provider extended outage (full provider, multi-day)

- **Workaround**: do we have multi-cloud, or do we wait?
- **Alternative communication**: how customers reach us if our primary channels are down
- **Estimated RTO**: 
- **Known gaps**: 

### 2.3 Data corruption / ransomware

- **Detection**: how we identify and isolate
- **Backup integrity**: when's the last verified-restorable backup
- **Restore process**: documented and tested
- **Customer communication**: what we'd say
- **Legal / regulatory steps**: required notifications, evidence preservation
- **Estimated RTO**: 
- **Known gaps**: 

### 2.4 Critical vendor failure (e.g., payment processor, identity provider)

- **Identification**: which vendors are critical
- **Workaround**: degraded mode, manual processing, alternative vendor
- **Comms with customers**: 
- **Estimated RTO**: 
- **Known gaps**: 

### 2.5 Loss of key personnel

- **Single points of failure**: who knows what only one person knows
- **Documentation gap**: what isn't documented that should be
- **Bus factor by area**: critical areas with bus factor 1
- **Mitigation in flight**: 

### 2.6 Office / facility loss (if applicable)

- **Remote-work readiness**: can the team operate fully remote
- **Hardware redundancy**: 
- **Network access from elsewhere**: 

---

## Part 3: Backup and recovery infrastructure

### 3.1 Backup inventory

| System | Backup method | Frequency | Retention | Last verified-restorable |
|--------|---------------|-----------|-----------|--------------------------|
|        |               |           |           |                          |

### 3.2 Restore tested

For each critical system, when did we LAST actually restore from backup?

- **Tested with full restore**: list with date
- **Never tested**: list (the most concerning row of the matrix)

> Untested backups are not backups. They are hopeful files.

---

## Part 4: Communication plan

### 4.1 Who to notify per scenario

| Scenario | Internal team | Exec | Customers | Public | Legal | Regulators |
|----------|---------------|------|-----------|--------|-------|------------|
|          |               |      |           |        |       |            |

### 4.2 Communication channels

- **Primary**: standard channels (status page, customer email)
- **Secondary** (if primary is impacted): backup channels (e.g., social media, alternate domain)
- **Internal coordination**: backup chat platform if Slack is down

---

## Part 5: Testing schedule

DR plans rot quickly. Test schedule:

- **Tabletop exercise**: quarterly (90 min, scenario-based, document gaps)
- **Backup restore test**: quarterly (actually restore a backup to a sandbox, verify integrity)
- **Failover drill**: semi-annually (real failover for a non-critical service)
- **Full DR test**: annually (the big one; coordinated with stakeholders)

---

## Part 6: Gap register

From the most recent test / review:

| Gap | Severity | Owner | Mitigation | Target date |
|-----|----------|-------|------------|-------------|
|     |          |       |            |             |

---

## Part 7: Approval and review

- **Approved by**: 
- **Date**: 
- **Distribution list** (who has access to this plan): 
- **Out-of-band copy** (where this plan lives if our systems are down): 

---

## Common failure modes

- **Backups never tested**: the most common DR failure. The first time you discover the backup is corrupt or incomplete is when you need to restore.
- **RTO/RPO not honest**: "1 hour RTO" written down, untested, and impossible. Test before you commit.
- **Dependency on the team that's also affected**: DR plan that assumes the team is available, when the disaster scenario is "the team's office is gone."
- **No out-of-band copy of the DR plan**: the plan lives in the system that's down.
- **Bus factor 1 areas not addressed**: the plan assumes someone knows how to recover X; that someone is on vacation.
- **Tabletop becomes performative**: read-aloud session with no real critique. Use a facilitator who isn't the plan owner; assign gaps with owners and dates.
- **Plan never updated after architecture changes**: a year-old DR plan for the architecture from 18 months ago.

---

## Related artifacts

- `incident-review-packet` — for individual incident response
- `risk-register` — for the longer list of risks the DR plan addresses
- `service-ownership-template` — for clarifying who owns DR for each service
- `dr-bcp-pack` (prompts) — for tabletop facilitation and post-test gap synthesis

templates.title: Major-Incident Exec Comms Template
templates.summary: Structured exec-facing communication for P0/P1/SEV1 incidents — first notification, periodic updates, resolution comms, post-incident summary, and customer-facing drafts. Calm, specific, honest; commit to next update rather than resolution time.
templates.why_it_matters: During a major incident, the comms are often what determine how the incident is remembered. Slow first notification, speculation as fact, optimistic resolution times, and silent missed updates erode trust permanently. This template gives the manager the structure to communicate with discipline when the room is on fire.
when_to_use: P0/P1/SEV1 incident declared and exec notification needed within 15–30 minutes
During active incident — periodic updates every 30–60 minutes
At resolution — final notification with initial root cause
24 hours later — exec summary for those who need the picture
not_for: Lower-severity incidents (use standard incident comms; this is for major-incident discipline)
Replacing the customer-facing status page workflow
Public PR comms without coordination with PR/Comms team
source_inspiration: Google SRE — incident response and customer communication
PagerDuty Incident Response docs
Atlassian incident management handbook
John Allspaw — Learning from Incidents

templates.template_body_markdown:
# Major-Incident Exec Comms Template

## How to use

This template is for exec-facing communication during and after a P0/P1/SEV1 incident — when the CEO, CFO, board, or major customers need to know what's happening, what we're doing, and what's true now.

It's NOT the team's incident channel. It's NOT the customer-facing status page (though it informs it). It's the structured comms that go to people who don't have the time or context to read the incident channel.

The principles:
- **Calm, specific, honest.** No platitudes; no panic.
- **Lead with current state.** Not the timeline. Execs need to know what's true RIGHT NOW.
- **What we know vs. what we suspect.** Always distinguish.
- **Commit to the next update time, not the resolution time.** Resolution is uncertain; the next update is a commitment you can hold.

---

## Part 1: First exec notification

> Sent within 15–30 minutes of P0/P1 declaration. The exec hears from you BEFORE they hear from a customer or the press.

### Template

**Subject**: [SEV1] [Service / area] — first notice

**Body**:

```
Status: SEV1 declared at [time UTC] — incident in progress.

Current scope:
- Affected: [user count / geography / service]
- Customer-visible impact: [what users experience right now]

What we know:
- [Confirmed fact 1]
- [Confirmed fact 2]

What we don't yet know:
- Root cause is still being determined
- Full scope of impact is being assessed

What we're doing:
- [Active mitigation, in plain language]
- Incident commander: [name]

Next update: [specific time, typically 30–60 min later]

[Your name], [Your role]
```

### Distribution

- Direct line manager (typically VP of Eng → CTO → CEO chain)
- Customer-facing leadership (Sales VP, CS VP, Support lead)
- Comms / PR if customer-visible
- Legal if data is involved

---

## Part 2: Periodic updates (every 30–60 minutes during active incident)

### Template

**Subject**: [SEV1] [Service / area] — Update [N] at [time]

**Body**:

```
Status: SEV1 — [active / mitigating / monitoring]
Time since start: [duration]

Current scope:
- Affected: [user count / geography / service — has this changed?]
- Customer-visible impact: [current state]

What we know now (changes from last update):
- [New confirmation 1]
- [Changed assessment]

What's still unknown:
- [Remaining unknowns]

Mitigation in progress:
- [Specific actions, who's doing what]
- [Expected timing for any in-flight action]

Customer comms status:
- Status page: [current message + last updated]
- Direct customer outreach: [tier-1 customers contacted: yes/no]
- Public comms (PR/social): [needed: yes/no/escalating]

Next update: [specific time]

[Your name]
```

### Tone notes

- If the situation is bad, say so. Not "experiencing some issues" when checkout is down for 60% of users.
- If you don't know the cause yet, say so explicitly. Don't speculate as fact.
- Avoid optimistic resolution timing. Commit to the next UPDATE, not the resolution.

---

## Part 3: Resolution comms

> Sent when the incident is fully resolved (not just mitigated).

### Template

**Subject**: [SEV1 RESOLVED] [Service / area]

**Body**:

```
Status: RESOLVED at [time UTC]
Total duration: [start to fully resolved]

What happened:
[2–3 sentences in plain language]

Customer impact:
- [Specific scope: # users, regions, services]
- [Revenue / SLA implications if applicable]
- [Customer comms sent: status page, direct outreach, public]

Initial root cause (subject to postmortem):
[What we know now; mark as initial]

What's still open:
- Postmortem due [date]
- Action items (initial list): [3–5 highest-priority]
- Customer follow-up: [what we owe customers]

[Your name]
```

---

## Part 4: Post-incident exec summary (24 hours after resolution)

> A more detailed summary for execs who need the picture without reading the postmortem.

### Template

**Subject**: [SEV1 SUMMARY] [Service / area] — [date]

**Headline** (2 sentences): What happened, current status, customer impact.

**Timeline** (compressed, key events with timestamps):
- [Detection]
- [Escalation]
- [Mitigation start]
- [Mitigation effective]
- [Full resolution]

**Customer impact** (specific):
- Users affected: 
- Geography: 
- Services: 
- SLA impact: 
- Revenue impact (if quantifiable): 
- Top-tier customers contacted: 

**Initial root cause**:
[What we know now; postmortem will sharpen]

**Why detection took [X] minutes**:
[Honest about detection lag if material]

**Why mitigation took [X] minutes**:
[Honest about mitigation lag if material]

**Immediate corrective actions taken**:
1. 
2. 
3. 

**Postmortem**:
- Scheduled: [date]
- Lead: [name]
- Will cover: full timeline, root cause, action items, patterns vs. recent incidents
- Will be shared with: [audience]

**What we're committing to**:
- [Specific commitments to leadership / customers / board]

**[Your name]**

---

## Part 5: Customer-facing comms (drafts for each channel)

### Status page

**During**:
> [Service] — investigating elevated [error rate / latency] affecting [scope]. Updates every [N] minutes.

**Resolved**:
> [Service] — issue resolved at [time]. [One-sentence on cause]. Postmortem to follow within 7 days.

### Tier-1 customer email (CSM-driven)

> [Customer name],
> 
> I wanted to reach out personally to let you know about an incident that affected [their use of the service] from [start] to [end]. 
> 
> Specifically: [what they experienced]. 
> 
> The cause was [plain-language summary]. We have [mitigated / fixed] this, and we're conducting a full postmortem this week. I will share that postmortem with you once complete and walk through the prevention work we're committing to.
> 
> If you'd like a call to discuss, please let me know — I'm happy to set up time with [our engineering lead].
> 
> [CSM / executive name]

### Public comms (PR, social — only if customer-visible at scale)

Coordinate with PR/Comms team. Default tone:
- Acknowledge the issue specifically, not vaguely
- Apologize for the impact, not for "any inconvenience"
- State what you're doing to prevent recurrence
- Don't over-promise

---

## Common failure modes

- **Slow first notification**: execs hear about the incident from a customer, the press, or social media before they hear from engineering. Loss of trust is permanent.
- **Speculation as fact**: stating a suspected cause as confirmed; later having to retract. Distinguish "what we know" from "what we suspect" every time.
- **Optimistic resolution time**: "We expect this resolved in 10 minutes" — repeated three times across two hours. Commit to the next update, not the resolution.
- **Update silence**: missing the committed next-update time without notice. If you can't update at the committed time, send a 1-line note that you're delaying the update.
- **Different stories to different audiences**: tier-1 customer hears one thing, the board hears another. Single source of truth.
- **No postmortem follow-through**: promised postmortem in 7 days; delivered in 28. Erodes the credibility of the original comms.

---

## Related artifacts

- `incident-summary-pack` (prompts) — for drafting status updates and post-incident summaries
- `incident-review-packet` — for the full postmortem after resolution
- `escalation-brief` — for non-incident escalations
- `postmortem-action-tracker` — for following through on the action items

templates.title: Service Deprecation Plan
templates.summary: Structured 6–18 month plan for retiring an internal service or public API: pre-announcement adoption inventory, public announcement, migration support, phased shutdown (soft → brownout → read-only → EOL → cleanup), and risk register.
templates.why_it_matters: Most failed deprecations fail at comms and migration support, not the technical shutdown. Killing a used service generates outages, escalations, and quiet rebellion from teams that never migrated. This template structures the whole arc so the deprecation actually lands.
when_to_use: Retiring an internal service with material adoption (>1 team, >1000 req/day)
Sunsetting a public API
Decommissioning a product capability with active users
Replacing one service with another that requires user migration
not_for: Deleting unused or zero-adoption services (just delete and announce)
Cases where you're not committed to the deprecation (a slipping EOL date erodes trust permanently)
When the replacement isn't ready (don't announce until it is)
source_inspiration: Google — public API deprecation policy patterns
Stripe — deprecation handling for public APIs
Will Larson — service migration patterns at scale

templates.template_body_markdown:
# Service Deprecation Plan

## How to use

This template is for the structured shutdown of an internal service, public API, or product capability that is being retired. Done well, deprecation is a 6–18 month exercise that respects users, preserves history, and frees engineering capacity. Done badly, it creates outages, customer escalations, and quiet rebellion from teams who never migrated.

Most failed deprecations failed at the comms and migration-support stage, not the technical shutdown. This template structures both.

This is for capabilities with material adoption (>1 customer, >1 internal team, >1000 requests/day or similar threshold). For unused services, just delete and announce.

---

## Header

- **Service / capability being deprecated**: 
- **Owning team**: 
- **Owner of the deprecation**: 
- **Why deprecating**: 
- **Replacement** (if any): 
- **Target end-of-life date**: 
- **Total deprecation timeline** (typically 6–18 months for material services): 

---

## Part 1: Pre-announcement

### 1.1 Decision and rationale

- **Why this service is being deprecated**: strategic shift, replaced by better option, no longer needed, cost too high, security/compliance issue
- **Decision-maker and approval date**: 
- **Cost-benefit summary**: cost of operating × remaining time vs. cost of migration support × remaining time

### 1.2 Adoption inventory

Before announcing, you need an honest read on usage:

- **Internal teams using this service**: list with primary contact
- **External customers using this service** (if external): list with their CSM / account team
- **Public API consumers** (if applicable): rough count, top consumers identified
- **Critical use cases**: what would break for which user if this just disappeared
- **Edge cases / unusual integrations**: what's likely to surprise you on the way out

### 1.3 Replacement readiness

If the deprecation has a replacement (a new service, a third-party tool, a manual workaround):

- **Replacement is ready for adoption**: yes / no / not yet
- **Migration documentation exists**: yes / no
- **Migration tooling exists**: yes / no
- **Performance / capability gaps in replacement**: explicit list
- **What we're committing to add to the replacement before deprecation**:

> If the replacement isn't ready, **don't announce yet**. Announcing too early makes the team look unreliable.

---

## Part 2: Public announcement

### 2.1 Announcement timing

- **Target announcement date**: 
- **Lead time before end-of-life**: typically 12 months for public APIs, 6 months for internal services with adoption, 3 months for narrow use
- **Announcement channels**: status page, blog post, email to known users, in-product notice

### 2.2 Announcement content

```
We are deprecating [service] effective [end-of-life date].

[1–2 sentences on why]

What this means for you:
- [Action you need to take]
- [Timeline for migration]
- [Where to get help]

Replacement:
- [What to use instead]
- [Documentation link]
- [Migration support available]

Timeline:
- [Date]: deprecation announced (today)
- [Date]: warnings added to service responses (e.g., HTTP deprecation header, log warnings)
- [Date]: new accounts / integrations no longer accepted
- [Date]: read-only mode (if applicable)
- [Date]: end-of-life

Support:
- Migration help: [contact / channel]
- Questions: [contact / channel]
- Office hours: [if offered]
```

---

## Part 3: Migration support

### 3.1 Migration documentation

Required:
- **Why we're deprecating**: link to announcement
- **What you should do**: step-by-step migration guide
- **Mapping table**: old API endpoints / capabilities → new equivalents
- **Code samples**: before / after in common languages
- **Common pitfalls**: things people hit that aren't obvious

### 3.2 Migration tooling

Where possible:
- **Automated migration tool**: a script / CLI / library that migrates the user
- **Detection tool**: a tool that scans a codebase / config and identifies what needs to change
- **Sandbox**: a place to test the new service before committing

### 3.3 Migration support channels

- **Slack / chat channel**: monitored during business hours
- **Office hours**: weekly synchronous slot for people working through migration
- **Direct outreach**: for top users (especially external customers), proactive outreach by your team

### 3.4 Migration tracking

- **Adoption metric**: % of traffic on old vs new
- **User-list status**: per known user, migrated / in-progress / not-started
- **Weekly report**: surface to leadership; surface stuck users early

---

## Part 4: Phased shutdown

### Phase 1 — Soft deprecation (announce + warn)

- Service continues to work normally
- Deprecation header / warning logged on every request
- No new accounts or integrations accepted
- Migration tooling and documentation live

### Phase 2 — Brownout testing (typically 1–3 months before EOL)

- Periodic short outages (1 hour, weekly) to surface integrations that aren't migrated
- Used as a fire-drill for users; communicates urgency
- Schedule announced 30 days in advance

### Phase 3 — Read-only mode (typically 30 days before EOL)

- Service responds to reads but rejects writes
- Forces the last laggards to migrate
- Reads-only window is short; clear comms

### Phase 4 — End of life

- Service responds with "410 Gone" or equivalent for 30 days, with helpful redirect to migration docs
- After 30 days, infrastructure decommissioned

### Phase 5 — Cleanup

- Code removed from active codebases
- Infrastructure decommissioned (cloud, monitoring, on-call)
- Documentation archived (not deleted; URLs continue to redirect to the migration guide)
- Team rotates to next priority

---

## Part 5: Risk register

| Risk | Likelihood | Impact | Mitigation |
|------|-----------|--------|------------|
| Top users haven't migrated by EOL date |  |  |  |
| Replacement gap surfaces during migration |  |  |  |
| Surprise consumers (unknown to us) appear at brownout |  |  |  |
| Internal team blocks on missing capability in replacement |  |  |  |
| Customer escalation forces extension |  |  |  |

---

## Part 6: Common failure modes

- **Announcing before the replacement is ready**: erodes trust; users have nothing to migrate to.
- **Soft EOL date that keeps slipping**: each slip teaches users that deadlines aren't real; the next deprecation will land worse.
- **No proactive outreach to top users**: top users find out at brownout when they're already broken.
- **Migration docs that don't survive testing**: write the docs by having someone else migrate using only the docs; fix what's missing.
- **No brownout phase**: skipping brownouts means EOL day surfaces every integration you didn't know about; chaotic.
- **Letting one customer block EOL indefinitely**: at some point, the deprecation has to land. Negotiate a paid extension or accept the customer churn; don't run a service forever for one user.
- **Deleting documentation post-EOL**: people google for the API a year later. URLs should redirect to migration docs, not 404.

---

## Related artifacts

- `service-deprecation-pack` (prompts) — for drafting the announcement, customer outreach, and brownout comms
- `vendor-evaluation-matrix` — if the replacement is a buy decision
- `risk-register` — for the deprecation's specific risks
- `incident-summary-pack` (prompts) — for the inevitable incidents during brownout

templates.title: Conflict Mediation Memo
templates.summary: Working memo for the manager mediating a sustained escalated conflict between two parties (engineers, managers, teams, partners). Forces hearing both sides separately, distinguishing positions from interests, surfacing the structural cause, and designing a resolution both can sign off on.
templates.why_it_matters: Most failed mediations failed by treating the presented conflict as the actual conflict, mediating positions instead of interests, or designing resolutions that depend on sustained good will rather than structural change. This template forces the diagnostic discipline that produces resolutions that hold.
when_to_use: Conflict between two parties has been escalating for weeks or months
Prior informal interventions haven't worked
The conflict is affecting work, team morale, or customer outcomes
Both parties are willing to engage in mediation in good faith
not_for: Routine disagreements that will resolve themselves
Cases where one party has clearly violated policy (HR-led, not mediator-led)
Cases where one party is acting in bad faith (mediation requires good faith from both)
Sharing in full with either party — this is the mediator's working document
source_inspiration: Roger Fisher / William Ury — Getting to Yes (interests vs. positions)
Sheila Heen / Douglas Stone — Difficult Conversations
Standard organizational mediation frameworks

inputs_required.name: Both parties willing to engage
inputs_required.description: Mediation requires good-faith participation from both.

inputs_required.name: Time for separate hearings before joint session
inputs_required.description: Hearing simultaneously produces theater; sequential produces honesty.

inputs_required.name: Authority to act on resolution
inputs_required.description: Mediator can change scope, decision-rights, or structure if needed.

sections.name: Background
sections.purpose: What surfaced this; what's been tried.

sections.name: Side A perspective
sections.purpose: Heard privately, documented.

sections.name: Side B perspective
sections.purpose: Same depth, separately.

sections.name: Mediator's read
sections.purpose: What's actually going on (often different from either party).

sections.name: Resolution design
sections.purpose: Address interests, not positions; structural over behavioral.

sections.name: Joint mediation session
sections.purpose: Run the session to land the resolution.

sections.name: Follow-through
sections.purpose: Written summary, check-ins, escalation path.

sections.name: Common failure modes
sections.purpose: Pre-empt predictable mediation failures.

templates.estimated_time: Separate hearings: 2 hours; mediator synthesis: 2 hours; joint session: 90 min; follow-through: 3 months
templates.template_body_markdown:
# Conflict Mediation Memo

## How to use

This template is for the manager (or the manager-of-managers) mediating a sustained, escalated conflict between two parties — engineers, managers, teams, or cross-functional partners. It's NOT for routine disagreements that resolve themselves; it's for conflicts that have been ignored long enough to require formal mediation.

The structure forces the mediator to:
- Hear both sides separately first, document what they actually want
- Distinguish positions (what they're asking for) from interests (what they actually need)
- Surface the structural causes underneath
- Design a resolution both can sign off on

This is a sensitive document. Treat it as confidential. Share specific portions with each party only with their consent or in the joint mediation session.

---

## Header

- **Mediator**: 
- **Date opened**: 
- **Parties**: 
- **Brief description of the conflict**: 
- **History**: how long this has been escalating, prior interventions
- **Stakes** (if unresolved): work stoppage, attrition, customer impact

---

## Part 1: Background

### 1.1 What surfaced this to you

- Who escalated to you
- What happened that made it formal (a Slack incident, a missed commitment, a team escalation)
- What had been tried before

### 1.2 Past interventions

- Conversations that have happened between the parties
- Manager-led 1:1s with each
- Anything that worked temporarily

---

## Part 2: Side A perspective

> Hear them privately first, before any joint conversation. Document what they say.

- **Position** (what they're asking for): 
- **Interests** (what they actually need to feel resolved): 
- **What they say happened**: 
- **What they say the other party did**: 
- **What they say their own contribution was** (or wasn't): 
- **What outcome they want**: 
- **What outcome they could live with**: 
- **What would be unacceptable**: 

---

## Part 3: Side B perspective

> Same depth, separately. Document.

- **Position**: 
- **Interests**: 
- **What they say happened**: 
- **What they say the other party did**: 
- **What they say their own contribution was** (or wasn't): 
- **What outcome they want**: 
- **What outcome they could live with**: 
- **What would be unacceptable**: 

---

## Part 4: Mediator's read

### 4.1 What's actually going on

The conflict as the mediator sees it (often different from either party's framing):

- **The presented conflict**: what they say it's about
- **The underlying conflict**: what it's actually about (often: scope ambiguity, status / recognition, trust deficit from a past event, structural contention for resources)
- **The structural cause**: organization-level factors that make this conflict more likely (unclear ownership, dual reporting, shared resource constraint)

### 4.2 Where the parties are right

Both parties almost always have a legitimate point. Document:

- **Side A is right about**: 
- **Side B is right about**: 

### 4.3 Where each is overstating

- **Side A is overstating**: 
- **Side B is overstating**: 

### 4.4 Truth they're both avoiding

Common in escalated conflicts: a truth neither party wants to name (their own contribution, an upstream decision that's the real issue, a fact about the work itself). Surfacing this is often the unlock.

---

## Part 5: Resolution design

### 5.1 What both parties need

Based on interests (not positions), what would resolve this:

- **For Side A**: 
- **For Side B**: 
- **For the team / org**: 

### 5.2 Proposed resolution

A specific, actionable resolution:

- **What changes**: scope, decision-rights, process, reporting
- **What both parties commit to**: behavior, communication
- **What the mediator commits to** (manager / leadership actions): 
- **Acknowledgment** of what's happened: where appropriate

### 5.3 Why this resolution should work

- Addresses Side A's interest because: 
- Addresses Side B's interest because: 
- Addresses the structural cause because: 

### 5.4 What this resolution does NOT do

Honest about what's unresolved or accepted:

- 
- 

---

## Part 6: Joint mediation session

> Once you've drafted the resolution, run the joint session to land it.

### 6.1 Pre-session prep

- Brief each party on the structure of the session
- Confirm both have committed to participating in good faith
- Set ground rules

### 6.2 Session agenda (typically 60–90 min)

1. **Opening** — mediator frames the session, the goal, the ground rules (5 min)
2. **Each party speaks** — what they want from the session, in their own words (15 min total)
3. **Mediator's read** — share Part 4 truthfully but with care (10 min)
4. **Resolution discussion** — propose Part 5; iterate based on response (30 min)
5. **Commitment** — what each party (and you) commits to; in writing (10 min)
6. **Follow-up cadence** — when we check back, what we'll be watching for (5 min)

### 6.3 Ground rules

- One person speaks at a time
- Statements about behavior, not character ("when you did X" not "you are X")
- The mediator is not a judge; the goal is resolution, not rightness
- Anything not agreed in the room is not committed
- Confidentiality of the session itself (what's said stays with the parties + mediator)

---

## Part 7: Follow-through

### 7.1 Written summary (within 24 hours)

To both parties: agreed resolution, commitments, follow-up date.

### 7.2 Follow-up check-ins

- **2 weeks**: separate 1:1 with each — is the resolution holding?
- **6 weeks**: joint check-in if needed
- **3 months**: assessment — is this stable?

### 7.3 If the resolution doesn't hold

- **Escalation path**: one party wasn't acting in good faith, or the structural cause wasn't addressed
- **Next intervention**: more formal (HR, role change, separation)
- **When to call it**: don't iterate forever; some conflicts need decisive structural action

---

## Common failure modes

- **Mediator hears both sides at once first**: produces theater, not honesty. Hear separately, then jointly.
- **Position-based mediation**: trying to find a middle on what they're asking for. Doesn't address what they need.
- **Avoiding the structural cause**: a personality conflict often masks a structural problem. Ignoring it means the same conflict recurs with different people.
- **Resolution that requires sustained good will**: brittle. Resolutions should change structure, not just behavior.
- **No follow-through**: the resolution holds for 2 weeks then unravels because no one checked in.
- **Escalating to HR too early or too late**: HR is the right partner for some cases; not all. Get HR perspective on whether they should be involved.

---

## Related artifacts

- `difficult-conversations-pack` (in difficult-conversations prompts) — for the individual conversations
- `manager-quality-review` — if the conflict involves a manager whose effectiveness is in question
- `org-design-proposal` — if the conflict reveals a structural issue requiring redesign
- `conflict-mediation-pack` (prompts) — for facilitating the mediation itself

templates.title: Distributed-First Operating Norms
templates.summary: Living document of operating norms for a distributed-first engineering organization: communication (async-default, response times, working-hours protection), meetings, decision-making, performance-and-growth fairness, hiring, connection, and explicit anti-patterns.
templates.why_it_matters: Distributed-first works when norms are explicit. It fails when norms are unwritten and people improvise — which means the loudest voices and timezone-dominant office set culture by default. This template makes the implicit explicit.
when_to_use: Establishing operating norms when shifting to distributed-first
Onboarding new hires (reference doc)
Resolving disputes about meeting cadence or response expectations
Aligning new managers on the org's operating model
Annual review and refresh
not_for: Hybrid teams trying to do remote occasionally (different operating model)
Single-location teams that occasionally have someone remote
Replacing actual culture work — the doc supports culture, doesn't substitute
source_inspiration: GitLab Handbook — distributed operating model
Automattic — distributed company practices
Basecamp — Shape Up and async-first patterns

templates.template_body_markdown:
# Distributed-First Operating Norms

## How to use

This template structures the operating norms for an engineering organization that is distributed-first — geographically distributed teams, no central HQ, async-default communication. It's NOT for hybrid teams trying to do remote occasionally; it's for orgs whose default is distributed and asynchronous.

Distributed-first works when the operating norms are explicit. It fails when the norms are unwritten and people improvise — which means the loudest voices and the timezone-dominant office set culture by default.

This template is intended as a living document, owned by engineering leadership, reviewed annually. It's referenced when onboarding new hires, resolving disputes about meeting cadence or response expectations, and aligning new managers.

---

## Header

- **Owner**: 
- **Last reviewed**: 
- **Next review**: 
- **Org timezones represented**: 
- **Number of distinct geographies**: 

---

## Part 1: Communication norms

### 1.1 Async-by-default

- **Decisions are made in writing**: in a designated decision doc / RFC / channel, not in a meeting
- **Discussions live in async channels**: with thread depth, summarized weekly
- **Synchronous time is reserved for**: real-time collaboration, hard conversations, relationship-building, ambiguity resolution
- **Sync time should be exceptional, not default**: the burden of justification is on synchronous, not async

### 1.2 Response time expectations

| Channel | Expected response time | Example |
|---------|------------------------|---------|
| Email |  |  |
| Slack DM |  |  |
| Slack channel |  |  |
| PR review |  |  |
| Async-decision document |  |  |
| Tagged-mention urgent |  |  |

> Make these explicit. People will assume something. Make sure they assume what's true.

### 1.3 Working-hours protection

- **Core hours** (if any): a window where overlap exists for sync coordination
- **No-meeting blocks**: protected time for deep work
- **Off-hours expectations**: explicit. No expectation of response after hours. If on-call, separate norm.
- **Holidays / time off**: respected across geographies (different countries have different calendars)

### 1.4 Documentation as default

- **Documentation is the source of truth**, not memory or chat
- **Decisions are written down**: ADRs, decision docs, meeting outputs
- **Onboarding-grade docs**: a new hire can ramp without a 1:1 explanation for most things
- **Doc maintenance**: stale docs are worse than missing ones; quarterly doc review

---

## Part 2: Meeting norms

### 2.1 When to have a meeting

A meeting is justified when:
- Real-time collaboration is required (paired debugging, design sketching)
- The conversation requires nuance that text loses (hard feedback, conflict resolution)
- Multiple parties need to align in a bounded time
- Trust-building requires presence

A meeting is NOT justified for:
- Status updates (write them down)
- FYI broadcasts (write them down)
- Decisions that could be async (write them down)

### 2.2 Meeting structure

- **Pre-read required**: every meeting has a doc circulated >24h in advance
- **Agenda set**: in the doc, with time per item
- **Recorded**: when participants can't attend live (with consent)
- **Notes captured**: decisions, action items, owners; circulated within 24h
- **Recurring meetings reviewed quarterly**: do they still produce signal?

### 2.3 Timezone fairness

- **Rotation of meeting times**: if a recurring meeting forces some attendees into off-hours, rotate
- **No 'always-the-same-region-suffers' meetings**: the cost of sync time is paid by everyone equitably over time
- **Async alternatives offered**: people who can't attend get the doc + opportunity to comment async with same weight

---

## Part 3: Decision-making norms

### 3.1 Decision rights

- **Single-threaded ownership**: every decision has one owner who decides
- **Consultation explicit**: who must be consulted before; not 'whoever happens to see the message'
- **Disagreement window**: an explicit period when stakeholders can object before a decision is final
- **Disagree-and-commit**: once decided, even disagreers commit

### 3.2 Decision documents

- **Format**: standardized (ADR, decision memo, RFC — pick one)
- **Required sections**: context, options considered, recommendation, decision-maker, deadline
- **Storage**: searchable, linked from related docs
- **Lifecycle**: status (draft / under review / decided / superseded) maintained

---

## Part 4: Performance and growth in distributed teams

### 4.1 Visibility without performative work

- **Outcomes over hours**: measured by what's shipped, not when people are online
- **Async demos**: video / Loom for showing work; not always live demos
- **Visibility is the manager's job to surface**: don't expect distributed engineers to self-promote
- **No bias toward visible-online people**: explicit attention to people who do their best work in their own time

### 4.2 Coaching and growth

- **1:1s are sacred**: video preferred for distributed reports; never canceled lightly
- **Career conversations require real time**: not async
- **Sponsorship is harder distributed**: managers must work harder to introduce reports to opportunities they wouldn't see

### 4.3 Promotion and review fairness

- **Reviews informed by written artifacts**: not 'who I see in the office'
- **Calibration explicitly addresses location bias**: don't let HQ-located people dominate promotion calibration
- **Track promotion rate by geography**: surface inequity

---

## Part 5: Hiring norms

### 5.1 Distributed hiring

- **Loop designed for async**: take-home or async-friendly options
- **Time-zone-fair scheduling**: don't always make candidates in a specific timezone work off-hours
- **Distributed onboarding**: assumes the new hire is solo in a city; doesn't depend on office presence

### 5.2 Geographic pay

- **Pay band by geography**: if applicable, transparent
- **Cost-of-living adjustments**: explicit
- **Equal pay for equal work**: defended explicitly when challenged

---

## Part 6: Connection and trust

### 6.1 Building relationships across distance

- **Onboarding includes relationship-building**: dedicated 1:1s with team members, structured intros
- **Periodic in-person time**: when, who pays, what we do
- **Async social channels**: optional; never compulsory
- **Cross-team rotation / collaboration**: structured ways for engineers to know each other across teams

### 6.2 Conflict resolution distributed

- **Sync time required for hard conversations**: not Slack
- **Mediator role explicit**: when a conflict goes beyond the team
- **Conflict is harder to detect distributed**: managers must work to surface what would be visible in an office

---

## Part 7: What this org does NOT do

Explicit anti-patterns for this org:

- 
- 
- 

Examples (per company): "We do not have an HQ that gets primary input"; "We do not require always-on Slack"; "We do not promote based on visibility to leadership"; "We do not penalize timezone-mismatch in reviews"; "We do not have meetings that exclude any major timezone consistently."

---

## Common failure modes

- **Norms unwritten**: defaults form anyway. Loudest voices and the timezone-dominant office set culture by default.
- **Async-first in name only**: leadership says async-first but actually decides in synchronous meetings the few timezone-aligned attendees can join.
- **No meeting review**: meetings accrete; calendar fills; deep work disappears.
- **Documentation rot**: writing is required but never maintained; new hires can't trust what they read.
- **Visibility bias**: HQ-located or timezone-aligned engineers get promoted faster despite identical contribution.
- **Holiday / vacation insensitivity**: scheduling launches on Thanksgiving (US-fine, EU-fine, but Lunar New Year is unconsidered).
- **'Periodic in-person' that's actually mandatory**: distributed-first promised, then 4x/year mandatory in-person makes it false.

---

## Related artifacts

- `leadership-cadence-pack` (prompts) — for cadence design suited to distributed teams
- `meeting-audit-template` — for the recurring quarterly review
- `engineering-health-review` — distributed teams need explicit health monitoring
- `distributed-team-pack` (prompts) — for distributed-team facilitation
